sentences.text: No, not really.
At that time, we loved Stalin because he was posted as the-- the grandfather [INAUDIBLE] of all the children and whatever.
And antisemitist was not allowed supposedly, and it was very hidden.
And I don't recall any incidents.
They were fine.
I don't recall any problems of coming home and crying because of that.
I haven't any problems.
I think-- excuse me-- I think they were afraid because the Russians were holding everybody, you know, really very strict.
And I mean, if you denounce somebody, they would have been either in jail, killed, or whatever, or sent to Siberia.
Because that's really what was happening to most of the people-- put to jail in Siberia.
Russian.
Oh, no, no.
Well, it's interesting because I remember-- and I was going to go back to that, too.
I remember when we were to Luck.
Now, as I said, my mother was kosher at home.
And the first time she had to eat pork, I remember like today, that she was gagging on it.
Because there was nothing else to be gotten.
I mean, you had to eat what you had to eat, what-- what was available.
You had no choice.
You had-- you were lucky if you got, you know, food altogether.
So that's far as the Jewish life was-- was gone.
If there was-- there probably was.
But I don't remember at that time that-- that that was on anybody's mind of the people that were-- that were running away, and they were called the [?
Brzeziny. ?] And you know, life was too tough and-- to think about, you know, things like that.
No.
No, he wasn't in trouble, except that, you know, we were afraid that they're going to-- to send them to-- to Siberia.
So you had to be-- really have-- you have-- you had to have your ears open and hear what's going on.
Well, he was considered a worker, however.
Yes, he was considered a worker.
Very loose contact--
--very loose.
I think towards the end, I think my mother did receive a few letters, but they were very scarce and very few.
And all we knew that they were taken to a ghetto.
The ghetto was created, and they were taken to a ghetto.
But that was it.
We didn't-- we really didn't know.
We didn't know about the concentration camps and whatever went on on the western part.
We had no idea, I mean, I personally.
Maybe my mother did know, but I doubt it because only when we came back to [?
Lodz ?] after the war, I started reading all the books that were out already at that time, and I-- I learned about the atrocities that were happening, you know, after-- during the war.
Yeah, some of them did.
One of my uncles did escape, but that must have been yet before the-- before the ghettos.
And he was missing his parents tremendously.
He went back and was killed.
No.
Well, people were coming.
Yes, people were running away.
And I guess they did hear the atrocities, but I-- my recollection, I-- I can't think about it.
I-- I don't remember.
I don't remember that-- that part.
From Luck, we went to Ludwipol.
Well, again, we rented an apartment in some Ukrainian's-- with some Ukrainian's family.
And I went to school.
My father got a job there.
And we led a so-so normal-- I should say normal, quote, 'normal' life, until 1941, where the war broke out.
Well, when-- when they came in, which was not-- not too long after the war broke out.
I don't remember.
No, I don't remember.
All I remember that my uncle, who was with us-- the one, the furrier-- he got the job with the Germans in-- in the German factory or whatever.
Because he was making furs, he knew how to-- to sew.
At that time, he became just a, you know, furrier, not a businessman.
And I guess the Germans-- it was very cold there so they needed fur coats and whatever.
So he was working.
They gave him a job.
He was working in one of the factories, so, or whatever.
No, there wasn't paid labor.
Maybe they gave them-- maybe they gave them a soup, you know, soup for that.
And-- and-- and I remember that they were taking us-- and me, I was what, at that time, maybe 1941, I was 10 years old-- we had to go on the street and pick cigarette butts.
And we had to-- to pull grass from in between the stones, like, they had the sidewalk, and there was, like, grass growing in between.
So the-- the-- the small children had to go and, you know, pick the thing.
And once, I remember, I was taken to a kitchen.
I guess it was a-- a German kitchen for-- for the soldiers.
And I guess we had to peel potatoes and scrub pots and wash dishes and-- and things like that.

I guess they-- they-- it is possible, now I'm not 100% sure, but it's possible that somebody from the Judenrate, you know, I-- I mean, that the Germans asked the Judenrate to deliver so many children or so many people or whatever.
And the Judenrate got-- you know, came to the houses and said, you, you, you, and you, you have to go to work, or you have to come and help or whatever.
That's how they were getting us.
Well, first of all, the names were enough for them to know we were Jewish.
And besides, I guess we had the-- all the Jews had to register.
Now, even-- even during the Russian times, when we were with the Russians, we were given like a-- it's not a passport, but it was like a document that looked almost like a passport.
And they ask what-- what was-- where you were born.
And if you were born in the western part of Poland, you were called this-- you were under certain paragraph.
So they knew if they wanted to-- the Russians-- if they wanted to send you-- to send all the refugees to-- to Russia, they would-- they-- it was just enough to look at that paragraph.
And there was the religion stated in the-- in those [INAUDIBLE].
So I think that we had to register when the Germans came in.
I think we had to register.
All the Jews probably had to go.
When they created that Judenrate, probably all the Jews had to go and register.
And then the house we were living in became part of a ghetto.
That was probably not too long after the Germans came in.
I don't remember how long.
Mm-hmm.
And at that time, I remember we had a two-- one bedroom and a living room, yes, and a kitchen.
And we were four people-- the four and my two uncles were with us at that time.
So we're six people.
Well, as far as I remember, all of a sudden, we ended up maybe 20 people in that apartment, because people that lived behind the borders of the ghetto had to come into the ghetto.
So we were very cramped and-- [SIGH] and had to give everybody, you know, had to make room for everybody.
So people were sleeping on the floors, on the-- wherever there was room.
And in the kitchen, you could find 20 people at the same time.
We didn't have to move--
--because, yeah, because we were in the ghetto.
Yes.
Yes, they had a fence around.
A tough wire, barbed wire, I think.
Barbed wire.
And where was the Judenrate in-- within the ghetto?
As far as I remember, yes.
Well, I guess people who thought they-- they can help themselves and make life a little bit more comfortable for themselves or bearable went into the Judenrate.
That I don't remember.
I don't remember.
I don't remember any names.
There probably was, but again, I don't remember.
Well, no, no, I don't.
Well, I guess there were different people in the Judenrate, too.
You know, there were people that tried to help others, and there were people probably that didn't.
And again, if you had some connection with somebody there, then-- and-- and they were nice to you, I guess you liked them.
And if they were-- but in general, I don't remember.
Well, it was-- the whole town was not that big.
But I would say it was a sizable community.
Probably.
But again, I don't remember.
I was only 10 years old, and I-- I really-- I remember I had friends, and I was playing with them.
And-- and I remember that sometimes during the night we would hear sirens and the German saying, Juden raus, Juden raus.
And every time that happened, we thought that that was the end of us.
These things I do remember.
I remember the sirens waking us up in the middle of the night.
No, no they were not.
All of a sudden, they would be over.
They would just scare the hell out of us.
And they didn't.
And if they did, maybe there were some certain people, and I don't remember that.
Yes.
Yes.
First, we had to wear like a band on our arm.
And then-- then we wore a yellow a yellow star.
I think it said Juden.
Well, my parents--
--while we were in Ludwipol, my parents developed a very close relationship with some Poles that lived not far-- not far away from us.
And they thought they were their friends, and maybe they were at that time.
I don't know.
And we lived in the ghetto for about-- from 19-- I guess-- 41 till probably-- actually, it was 1943-- about probably a year or maybe more.
And naturally, there was no school.
There was-- there was nothing.
I mean, everybody was just-- but one thing I remember.
It's interesting.
While in the ghetto, I think these Poles-- oh, that's why we thought they were friends-- these Poles or Ukrainians, whoever they were, they gave me a piece of land, you know, a little piece of land.
And I remember as a 10-year-old girl, I was tending to it, and I was growing tomatoes and corn and cucumbers and whatever.
And that was my daily routine.
That was in the ghetto, right.
And that was-- that was my-- my daily routine.
And-- and that's why we had cucumbers, and we had some vegetables, because I grew them.
Anyhow, my father-- my father was killed during the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clean-up, or whatever they call it.
Not [NON-ENGLISH], during the-- the [? thing ?] of the ghetto.
Right.
Oh, yes.
Well, what happened in-- in the morning, my father went out, I guess to see what's going on.
Because that was the thing-- to get up and to go out.

And he came running back home and said to my mother, take the children and go to these people-- to these Polish or Ukrainian people-- and stay with them, because I think there's going to be-- it looks like today there's going to be an aktion here.
And I'm going to see what's going on, and I'll follow you there and I'll meet you there.
So my-- my mother took me and my sister, just one light coat-- I remember like today-- a little grey coat.
And my sister was born in 1937, so she was-- she was five younger than me.
So she was like five years old at that time.
And we ran to these people.
We somehow got out and ran to these people and knocked at their doors.
And naturally, nobody opened the doors for us.
So-- and at that time already was a lot of tumult there.
There was a lot of going on.
And we-- she turned around from there and started just going ahead of herself, you know, just not knowing where she was going or what was going on.
No, away from the ghetto.
And all of a sudden, she saw like a little potato-- what do you call it?
It looked like a dog's house, really.
And she opened the door from it, and all of a sudden she saw some people in there.
And it turned out that it was like a potato-- not a dump, potato--
Like a potato cellar.
And when she opened it, there were some people already hidden in there.
So she pushed me.
She pushed my sister in there, and she jumped in there.
And while we were there, we found out from the other people, too, that the aktion was going on.
And that they-- and we would-- we stayed there in that cellar with our hearts on our shoulders, I have to tell you, because we heard everything that was going on.
We heard the Germans passing back and forth.
And we heard the squeaking and the screeching and the yelling and the-- and the German language and shooting and-- and all that.
And luckily, I don't know what happened, that some other people opened the door.
Some other people came in and were saved with us.
And we stayed there until the middle of the night.
In the middle of the night, thanks to the other people that were there, because my mother wouldn't have any idea where to go or what to do, we came out from there.
And we were not far from the Bug, from the River Bug.
And in the middle of the night, we crossed that River Bug.
And I guess my mother had probably held my sister somehow.
Thank god that the river was not really that deep.
And me, I don't remember.
Somebody probably-- maybe there was a young man there or whatever-- took me on his shoulders and crossed the River Bug.
When we crossed the River Bug, we ended up in the forest.
And walking in the forest for a couple of hours, all of a sudden, across, my mother saw his-- her brother walking, you know, walking the opposite way.
Well, you can imagine, I mean, that reunion, that there was a soul that, you know, family.

And that's how that started, hiding in the forest and going through this part of-- later on, while we were in forest and slept, you know, I mean, the-- the first, we slept on the ground and nothing to get changed in, and we were wet from the-- from the water and all that.
Then we found out how my father was killed, because somebody escaped from the mass gave.
It turned out that there was a mass grave that-- someplace on the outskirts of Ludwipol.
And apparently, they must have been taking the-- the Jews from the ghetto to dig these graves.
I didn't hear anything about it, but somebody either must have heard, [? though ?] people didn't speak about it because they were afraid to be shot maybe.
They-- they lined all these people, you know, at the-- the mass-- mass grave, and the German just shot all of them.
Well, this-- this man that was telling us this story was not shot.
His daughters next to him were shot.
And when he saw that, he-- he jumped.
He-- he-- he fell to the grave, too, I mean.
And that misled the-- the-- the Germans, because they thought he was dead.
In the middle of the night, he escaped-- he escaped the ghetto-- he escaped the-- the graves and whatever.
And I think he survived, but I have no idea.
I don't remember his name.
I don't know where they were-- I-- I know nothing about it, which is a shame.
So he told us that what had happened with my father, my father went to look for us to these people.
Well, he had the same experience as us.
So he went back to the apartment, went under the bed, and hid himself under the bed.
And he was there.
He survived.
He survived the cleaning out of the ghetto.
And at night, I think some Ukrainians or Poles came in to rob the apartment, and they found him there.
And they called the Germans, and the Germans took him back to the-- they brought him back to the mass grave, and he was killed there.
Yes.
Well, it was tough.
It was a lot of bickering and a lot of jealousy and a lot of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was tough.
It was tough.
Now, the people that were in the apartment originally had a little bit more, I guess, power, or they thought they had more power--
--right, than the people that were brought in.
But it was very tough because, you know, people were stealing food because the food was so scarce, you know.
And so it-- it was-- it was tough.
And you know, there you couldn't-- I mean, there were no bathrooms, so we had to go outside and shower outside.
And I think there were outhouses.
So the hygiene wasn't the greatest.
I remember once laying in bed, and I didn't feel like getting up next morning.
And I said to myself that-- the incidents, you somehow remember some.
And I'm laying in bed, I said, oh, I hope I'm sick today.
I hope I get sick so I don't have to get up.
I don't have to face the world.
I don't-- but what do you know?
I got up in the morning.
I had very high temperature.
So I think self-suggestion, you know, worked on you really-- What happened when--
[INAUDIBLE].
Well, there-- I guess there were some doctors that-- you-- you just reminded me of something, [? though. ?] There were some doctors that were attending.
And maybe you had some Poles on the outside, and maybe somebody would give you some medication.
Or you would-- you could get some medication from-- from the outside world.
And if you didn't, you died.
I don't remember.
I-- I know that there was, but, you know, I was so far away from it.
I didn't know about sex at that time yet, you know.
I mean, I only learned later on about it.
So I-- I really-- I-- I know I heard later on that there-- there were some babies born.
As a matter of fact, it's interesting.
There was a-- a man that was living with us in the apartment, and he was my father's very good friend.
But he had a crush on my mother.
And after the war, he wanted to marry my mother, because he survived.
But so, I guess there were-- besides, you know, all these problems and these whatever it is, I mean, there was still emotions and relationships and whatever probably formed.
No.
No.
I don't [? know. ?]
At that time, no.
I did take care of kids, but that was-- that was when we were in the forests already.
In the ghetto?
Well, maybe--
No, no.
Yeah.
No, I don't remember any organized.
I remember we used to spend time with some friends in each other's house, playing with dolls, playing doctor, playing house, I mean, you know, just regular whatever it is.
Then I guess, maybe going outside and playing maybe some ball or-- or whatever.
But I don't remember any organized activities.
I don't remember reading, [? because I-- ?] I mean, grown-ups maybe yes.
Maybe they did have some books or maybe they read.
But can't remember anything being done for the children.
I doubt it because I don't think we even had electricity there.
You see, this was a very-- like Radom was already, you know, civilized town, a city, whatever.
There, I think we used kerosene.
It was a really medieval times, [INAUDIBLE] you know.
I guess there were-- there must have been Russians.
I don't really remember that.
Maybe-- maybe my mother didn't look Jewish.
Maybe she could go out from the ghetto.
Maybe they did let people out for-- I-- I don't remember.
But they had to be Russians because, I mean, although the Russians were probably for the-- you know, for-- for the dogs.
I know that maybe my uncle was bringing something from the factory.
I know that when I was working at the kitchen at that time, I remember they gave me soup.
So maybe my father went out to work.
Maybe they gave him some.
I-- I don't remember what.
Well, the only money I think that we had was-- was that fur.
And maybe--
Yeah, we had all the fur with us, until-- yeah.
And as a matter of fact, when we were in Ludwipol, I think we didn't have-- we didn't have it in the ghetto.
We did-- can't remember.
We-- my mother had a big hole dug out, and they put the whole trunk of the fur, you know.
And after the war when we went there, my mother wanted to, you know, to find it and take some.
It was gone.
So somebody found it, and they dug it out.
I don't even--
--I don't even know if they had-- you know, I don't even-- that they had money.
All they had was-- they were working.
My father was working all along.
So I think there was enough to sustain ourselves, you know, during the Russians.
--but I don't think-- I don't know whether they had anything.
No.
I don't-- I don't remember.
In the ghetto, you mean?
I don't remember.
OK.
Can I--
